section.title: The Man of Lawlessness
section.sectionRationale: Someone has rattled the church with the claim that the Day of the Lord has already come. Paul corrects the error by teaching that before that day, the rebellion must come and the man of lawlessness must be revealed, opposing God and exalting himself, restrained for now until his appointed moment, and finally destroyed by the breath of the Lord's mouth. This is the letter's great prophetic and pastoral correction.
section.thesis: The Day of the Lord has not come and will not come until the great rebellion and the man of lawlessness are revealed; yet even this figure is restrained by God's timing and doomed to be destroyed by the mere breath of the returning Christ, so the church need not be shaken.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the correction of the false alarm about the Day of the Lord; the profile of the man of lawlessness, his self-exaltation, the present restraint, and his sure destruction; and the tragic account of those who perish because they refused to love the truth.

units.label: Do not be shaken
units.note: correcting the false alarm about the Day of the Lord

verses.text: Now concerning the {{parousia|coming}} of our Lord Jesus Christ and our being gathered together to Him, we ask you, brothers,
not to be easily disconcerted or alarmed by any spirit or message or letter seeming to be from us, alleging that the {{dayOfTheLord|Day of the Lord}} has already come.

units.label: The man of lawlessness revealed and restrained
units.note: self-exaltation, present restraint, and certain doom

verses.text: Let no one deceive you in any way, for it will not come until the {{apostasia|rebellion}} occurs and the man of {{anomia|lawlessness}}, the {{apoleia|son of destruction}}, is revealed.
He will oppose and exalt himself above every so-called god or object of worship. So he will seat himself in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God.
Do you not remember that I told you these things while I was still with you?
And you know what is now restraining him, so that he may be revealed at the proper time.
For the mystery of lawlessness is already at work, but the one who now {{katecho|restrains}} it will continue until he is taken out of the way.
And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will slay with the breath of His mouth and annihilate by the majesty of His {{parousia|arrival}}.

units.label: The perishing who refused the truth

verses.text: The {{parousia|coming}} of the lawless one will be accompanied by the working of Satan, with every kind of power, sign, and false wonder,
and with every wicked deception directed against those who are perishing, because they refused the love of the truth that would have saved them.
For this reason God will send them a powerful delusion so that they believe the lie,
in order that judgment may come upon all who have disbelieved the truth and delighted in wickedness.

parousia.gloss: Presence, arrival, coming, used in the ancient world for the official visit of a king or emperor. Paul applies it to the glorious coming of the Lord Jesus, but pointedly also to the coming of the lawless one in verse 9, setting up a dark parody. The true King's arrival will simply undo the counterfeit's.

dayOfTheLord.gloss: The Day of the Lord, the Old Testament expression for God's decisive intervention in history to save and to judge. The Thessalonians had been told it was already present; Paul insists it is still future, preceded by the rebellion and the revealing of the man of lawlessness. The phrase now centres on the coming of the Lord Jesus.

apostasia.gloss: Rebellion, defection, a falling away, the root of the English word apostasy. It names a decisive revolt against God that must occur before the Day of the Lord. Whether it points to a great turning from the faith, a final political-religious rebellion, or both, it marks a climax of organized opposition to God.

anomia.gloss: Lawlessness, the condition of being against or without law, a settled defiance of God's rule. The coming figure is the man of anomia, lawlessness personified and embodied. The same mystery of lawlessness, Paul says, is already secretly at work in the world, awaiting its full and open expression.

apoleia.gloss: Destruction, ruin, perdition. The man of lawlessness is called the son of destruction, a Hebrew idiom meaning one destined for and characterized by ruin, the same title Jesus gave Judas. It names both his nature and his certain end under God's judgment.

katecho.gloss: To hold back, restrain, hold down, or hold fast. Paul speaks of both a restraining thing, verse 6, and a restraining person, verse 7, now holding back the lawless one until the appointed time. The identity of this restrainer is one of the letter's famous puzzles, but its function is clear: God controls the timing.

basic.context: The church had been thrown into a panic by the claim, perhaps from a forged letter or a supposed prophecy, that the Day of the Lord had already arrived. Paul writes to calm them. That day, he says, will not come until two things happen: a great rebellion against God, and the appearance of a figure he calls the man of lawlessness, who exalts himself as God. For now something and someone are holding this figure back, and when he finally appears, the returning Lord Jesus will destroy him with a mere breath. So the Thessalonians should not be shaken.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul assumes his readers remember his earlier teaching, which is why some details are compressed and hard for us. Two honest puzzles remain: who or what is the restrainer, and exactly how and when the man of lawlessness appears. Christians have held different views on both. What Paul makes unmistakably clear is the main point: the day is future, evil is real but restrained by God, and Christ's victory over the lawless one is certain and easy.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole passage turns on the word parousia, the royal arrival, used both of Christ's coming, verse 1, and, as a dark parody, of the lawless one's coming, verse 9. The Day of the Lord, hemera tou kyriou, is the Old Testament day of God's decisive action. Before it comes the apostasia, the great rebellion, and the man of anomia, lawlessness embodied, also called the son of apoleia, destruction, one marked for ruin. For now the katecho, the restraint, holds him back until God's set time.
basic.typology: The man of lawlessness who seats himself in the temple proclaiming himself God takes up the pattern of Daniel's arrogant king and the abomination of desolation, and of every ruler who has deified himself. He is the final antitype of that self-exalting rebellion, and Christ, the true King, is his undoing.
basic.christ: Christ is the returning King whose arrival ends the counterfeit. The lawless one stages a parody of Christ's coming, with satanic power, signs, and false wonders, but the Lord Jesus will simply slay him with the breath of His mouth and annihilate him by the splendor of His coming. No struggle, no contest; the mere appearing of Christ is the victory.
basic.god: God is sovereign over evil and its timing. Even the mystery of lawlessness operates only within the limits God sets, restrained until the proper time. And God is just: those who refuse to love the truth are finally given over to the delusion they preferred, so that His judgment falls righteously on those who delighted in wickedness.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 11 describes a king who exalts himself above every god, the pattern behind the man of lawlessness. Jesus in Matthew 24 warns of false christs with signs and wonders and of the abomination in the holy place. And 1 John 2 speaks of antichrist, already at work in many, awaiting a final expression.
basic.oneStory: History runs toward a final showdown between the self-exalting rebellion of the man of lawlessness and the true King, Jesus. The mystery of lawlessness already at work will one day come to a head, but the outcome is never in doubt: at His coming Christ destroys the counterfeit with a breath, vindicating the truth and the people who loved it.

context.literary: The section is framed by the word coming, parousia, in verse 1 and again in verse 8, moving from the church's alarm, to the profile of the lawless one and the present restraint, to the fate of those who perish. It is tightly argued: because the day is preceded by named events, and those events have not occurred, the day has not come. Verse 5 reminds them this is not new teaching.
context.historical: First-century believers knew rulers who claimed divine honors, and the memory of attempts to profane the Jerusalem temple was fresh. A forged or misattributed letter, hinted at in verse 2 and answered in 3:17, could easily unsettle a young church. The pressure of persecution made an over-realized hope, that the day had already dawned, both tempting and destabilizing.
context.canonical: This is the New Testament's fullest single portrait of the final man of lawlessness, drawing on Daniel and standing alongside the Olivet Discourse and John's antichrist and beast imagery. It belongs to Scripture's consistent teaching that opposition to God will crest before the end and be crushed by the returning Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To stop the church from being shaken by the false claim that the Day of the Lord had come, by reminding them that the day is preceded by the rebellion and the revealing of the man of lawlessness, and by assuring them that this figure is restrained by God and doomed to be destroyed by Christ at His coming.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely descriptive prophecy, telling the church what must happen before the day. Its prescriptive force is the surrounding command: do not be quickly shaken or alarmed, and do not be deceived. The proper response to unsettling end-times claims is settled confidence in Christ's certain victory, not speculation or panic.

debates.question: Who is the man of lawlessness in verses 3 and 4?

views.label: A future individual, a final antichrist figure
views.case: The concrete language, one who is revealed, seats himself in the temple, and is destroyed at Christ's coming, points to a specific person at the end, the climactic embodiment of lawlessness.

views.label: A recurring pattern climaxing in a final figure
views.case: Since the mystery of lawlessness is already at work and many antichrists have come, some see a spirit expressed in many self-deifying rulers, coming to a head in a final manifestation; the two readings overlap in the last figure.

debates.bearing: Either way the church is warned to expect intensifying, self-exalting opposition to God and assured that Christ will destroy it effortlessly at His coming.

debates.question: Who or what is the restrainer in verses 6 and 7?

views.label: A providential ordering, such as government or God's own timing
views.case: The neuter what restrains in verse 6 suggests an institution or principle, like lawful authority that holds back chaos, or simply God's sovereign schedule keeping evil in check until the set time.

views.label: A personal agent, such as the Spirit or an angelic power
views.case: The masculine the one who restrains in verse 7 suggests a person; many propose the Holy Spirit working through the church, or a mighty angel like Michael, holding the lawless one back until removed.

debates.bearing: Paul does not settle the identity, and godly readers differ; the clear point is that God, not the lawless one, controls the timing, so the church can rest.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is built on parousia, the word for a king's official arrival: Paul names both the coming of the Lord Jesus, verse 1, and, in a deliberate parody, the coming of the lawless one, verse 9, so the counterfeit apes the true King. The alarm concerns the hemera tou kyriou, the Day of the Lord, the Old Testament day of saving judgment, which Paul insists is still future. Two things must precede it: the apostasia, the decisive rebellion that gives us the word apostasy, and the revealing of the man of anomia, lawlessness embodied, called the son of apoleia, destruction, a Hebrew idiom for one destined to ruin, the very title Jesus used of Judas. For now the katecho, both a restraining thing and a restraining person, holds the lawless one back until God's appointed moment, and then the Lord Jesus ends him with the breath of His mouth and the splendor of His parousia.

typology.type: The man of lawlessness in the temple claiming to be God
typology.fulfillment: Takes up Daniel's self-exalting king and the abomination of desolation, and every ruler who deified himself, brought to a head in the final lawless one whom Christ destroys.

typology.type: The parousia of the lawless one as counterfeit
typology.fulfillment: A satanic imitation of the true King's coming, with false signs and wonders, exposed and undone by the genuine parousia of the Lord Jesus.

study.christ: Christ is the true and returning King, and the whole passage measures the counterfeit against Him. The lawless one arrives with a false parousia, staging satanic power, signs, and lying wonders, and even enthrones himself as God. But when the real King appears, there is no battle: the Lord Jesus slays him with the breath of His mouth and abolishes him by the splendor of His coming. Christ's victory is so complete that His mere appearing is the end of evil's masterpiece, which is why the church can be utterly unshaken.
study.god: God is shown as sovereign and just. Evil is real, and the mystery of lawlessness is already at work, yet it operates only within God's restraint, revealed at the proper time He appoints. God is also righteous in judgment: to those who refuse the love of the truth He finally sends a powerful delusion, giving them over to the lie they preferred, so that judgment falls justly on all who delighted in wickedness rather than truth.

crossReferences.note: A king who exalts himself above every god and speaks against the God of gods, the pattern behind the self-deifying man of lawlessness.
False christs and false prophets performing great signs and wonders to deceive, paralleling the lawless one's lying signs.
The antichrist is coming, and already many antichrists have come, matching the mystery of lawlessness already at work.
The Messiah slaying the wicked with the breath of His lips, the very image Paul uses of Christ destroying the lawless one.

study.oneStory: This passage locates the church between the first and second comings of Christ, in an age where the mystery of lawlessness is already working but restrained. History is heading toward a final, open rebellion and a supreme counterfeit king, yet the story's end is certain: the true King returns and, with a breath, ends the imposter. God vindicates the truth and the people who loved it, and judges those who preferred the lie, bringing the long conflict between God's rule and human self-exaltation to its appointed close.

sermon.title: The Return of Christ
sermon.why: Preaching from 2 Thessalonians 2:1-2, Begg addresses the very alarm Paul answers here, urging believers not to be quickly shaken or troubled about the Day of the Lord but to rest in the certainty of Christ's return. A clear, pastorally steadying treatment of the opening of this movement.

quiz.q: What false claim had unsettled the Thessalonian church, according to verse 2?

options.text: That the Day of the Lord had already come.
options.note: Verse 2 names the alarming claim that the Day of the Lord was already present.

options.text: That Jesus had never really risen from the dead.
options.note: The issue here is the timing of the Day of the Lord, not the resurrection.

options.text: That Paul had abandoned them for good.
options.note: Paul is writing to steady them, not the concern raised in verse 2.

options.text: That the persecution would never end.
options.note: The specific alarm was about the Day of the Lord having come, though persecution is the backdrop.

quiz.q: According to verse 3, what must happen before the Day of the Lord comes?

options.text: The rebellion must occur and the man of lawlessness must be revealed.
options.note: Verse 3 names the rebellion and the revealing of the man of lawlessness.

options.text: Every nation must first become Christian.
options.note: Paul names rebellion and the lawless one, not global conversion, as the precursor.

options.text: The temple must be rebuilt in gold.
options.note: The lawless one seats himself in the temple, but Paul does not speak of a golden rebuilding as the sign.

options.text: Nothing at all; it comes with no warning signs.
options.note: Paul's whole point is that named events precede the day.

quiz.q: How does the man of lawlessness exalt himself in verse 4?

options.text: He opposes and exalts himself above every so-called god and proclaims himself to be God.
options.note: Verse 4 describes his self-deifying opposition to all worship.

options.text: He humbly serves in the temple as a priest.
options.note: He seats himself as God, the opposite of humble service.

options.text: He merely claims to be a great teacher.
options.note: He claims far more, proclaiming himself to be God.

options.text: He sets up rival kings in every nation.
options.note: The text focuses on his self-exaltation as God, not on installing other kings.

quiz.q: What does Paul say is happening now, before the lawless one is revealed, in verses 6 and 7?

options.text: Something and someone are restraining him until the proper time.
options.note: Verses 6 and 7 speak of a restraint holding him back until God's appointed moment.

options.text: The lawless one has already been destroyed.
options.note: His destruction is future, at Christ's coming; for now he is restrained.

options.text: Evil has completely vanished from the world.
options.note: The mystery of lawlessness is already at work, though restrained.

options.text: God has lost control of the timing.
options.note: The opposite; the restraint shows God controls the timing, even if the restrainer's identity is debated.

quiz.q: How will the lawless one finally be defeated, according to verse 8?

options.text: The Lord Jesus will slay him with the breath of His mouth and by the splendor of His coming.
options.note: Verse 8 shows Christ destroying him effortlessly at His coming.

options.text: A great army of believers will overpower him in battle.
options.note: The victory is Christ's alone, by a mere breath, not a human army.

options.text: He will simply grow old and fade away on his own.
options.note: He is destroyed by the returning Christ, not by natural decline.

options.text: The restrainer will defeat him before Christ returns.
options.note: The restrainer only holds him back; it is the Lord Jesus who destroys him at His coming.

quiz.q: Why do those who perish fall for the lawless one's deception, according to verses 10 to 12?

options.text: Because they refused to love the truth, so God gave them over to the delusion they preferred.
options.note: Verses 10 to 12 trace their ruin to refusing the love of the truth and delighting in wickedness.

options.text: Because they were never given any chance to know the truth.
options.note: The truth would have saved them; they refused to love it.

options.text: Because God delights in deceiving people.
options.note: The delusion is a just judgment on those who first rejected the truth, not divine malice.

options.text: Because the lawless one is stronger than God.
options.note: The lawless one works by satanic power, but he is doomed; the issue is their refusal of the truth.

questions: The Thessalonians were quickly shaken by an alarming message about the end. What unsettling claims or predictions tend to rattle your peace, and how does Christ's certain victory steady you?

Paul says the man of lawlessness will exalt himself even to the point of claiming to be God. Where do you see the pattern of self-exaltation, putting self in God's place, at work in the world and in your own heart?
The mystery of lawlessness is already at work but held back by God's restraint. How does knowing that God controls the timing of evil change the way you face a troubling age?
Christ defeats the great counterfeit with a mere breath. How does the sheer ease of His victory reshape your fears about the powers arrayed against the gospel?
Those who perish are described as refusing to love the truth. Is there any truth from God you are tempted to keep at arm's length, and what would it look like to love it instead?

translationNotes.note: The rebellion translates apostasia, a decisive falling away or revolt; the man of lawlessness, the son of destruction renders a Hebrew idiom for one whose nature and destiny is ruin. Em dashes in the source were rendered as commas per house style.

What is now restraining, neuter in verse 6, and the one who now restrains, masculine in verse 7, are famously unspecified; Paul assumes his readers know from his earlier teaching, and the identity remains debated.
Slay with the breath of His mouth echoes Isaiah 11:4, where the Messiah destroys the wicked by the breath of His lips, underscoring the effortlessness of Christ's victory.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Thessalonians 2:1-12. Em dashes in verse 3 rendered as commas per house style. All twelve verses covered contiguously.
